base_explanation.why: Control the center and open lines for the bishop and queen.
Develop the knight and attack the e5 pawn.
Defend the e5 pawn with the knight.
Prepare d4 to build a strong pawn center. Also opens the a4 diagonal for the queen.

variations.name: Main Line: 3...Nf6 4.d4 Nxe4 (Knight Capture)
variations.description: Black's most common and safest response. After 4.d4, Black grabs the e4 pawn, and White gains a space advantage with d5.
variations.result: White has a space advantage and active pieces; roughly equal but easier to play as White at beginner level.

moves_from_base.why: Attack the e4 pawn, the most natural and popular move.
Execute the plan behind c3 -- seize the center with two pawns.
Grab the free pawn. Black's most aggressive option.
Kick the c6 knight before recapturing on e5. Gains space and tempo.
The knight retreats to e7, the only good square that doesn't block development.
Recapture the pawn. White has recovered material and has a strong d5 wedge.
Develop the knight to an active square, attacking the e5 knight.
Develop the bishop to an active diagonal, eyeing the kingside. Also sets a trap (see trap sub-line).
Correct play -- develop the second knight and avoid the Nxf2 trap.
Castle to safety. White has a comfortable space advantage.

variations.full_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. c3 Nf6 4. d4 Nxe4 5. d5 Ne7 6. Nxe5 Ng6 7. Bd3 Nf6 8. O-O

sub_variations.name: Nxf2 Trap (Black falls into the trap)
sub_variations.description: Black sees the f2 pawn is only defended by the king and tries to win it. This is a famous trap where White wins at least a piece.
sub_variations.result: TRAP: White wins a piece (knight). Black's greed is punished.

moves_from_branch.why: MISTAKE: Black tries to fork the queen and rook, but it's a trap.
KEY MOVE: Instead of recapturing on f2, White attacks the other knight with a discovered threat. The f7 pawn is now under fire.
Black grabs the queen, thinking they're winning.
Check! The king must move.
Forced -- the only legal king move.
Another check, with discovered attack potential.
The king is dragged into the open.
Knight check, forking the king and threatening mate patterns.
The king is completely exposed in the center.
Threatens b4 checkmate. Black must give back the queen.

sub_variations.name: Positional Main Line: 7. Nxg6 (Exchange First)
sub_variations.description: White trades the e5 knight for Black's g6 knight first, then centralizes the queen. A solid positional approach that avoids any traps.
sub_variations.result: White has a pleasant positional advantage with the d5 wedge and better piece coordination.

moves_from_branch.why: Trade the knight before Black can use it. Opens the h-file for Black but simplifies to White's advantage.
Forced recapture.
Centralize the queen, targeting the e4 knight and preparing development.
Defend the knight and prepare to trade queens.

Open the diagonal for the c8 bishop.
Attack the e4 knight. White will regain the pawn with a better position.

variations.name: Main Line: 3...Nf6 4.d4 exd4 (Pawn Capture)
variations.description: Black captures on d4 instead of e4. White gets a strong pawn center after e5, kicking the knight.
variations.result: White has a big center with pawns on c3 and e5. Roughly equal with accurate play from Black, but White has easy development.

moves_from_base.why: Attack the e4 pawn.
Push forward in the center.

variations.name: Ponziani Countergambit: 3...d5 (Main Variation with 4...f6)
variations.description: Black strikes back in the center immediately. White's 4.Qa4 is the key move, pinning the knight and threatening e5.
variations.result: Complex middlegame. Black retains central space but must play accurately. Roughly equal.

moves_from_base.why: Counter-strike in the center, challenging White's e4 pawn.
KEY MOVE: Pin the c6 knight to the king. Now e5 is threatened since the knight can't defend it.
The Steinitz Variation -- defend e5 with the f-pawn. Surprisingly the best move here.
Pin the knight again and develop the bishop to an active square.
Unpin and prepare to reroute the knight. Defends c6.

sub_variations.name: 3...d5 Trap: The Bd6 Blunder
sub_variations.description: A common beginner mistake. Black plays Bd6 to defend e5, but this blocks the queen's defense of d5.
sub_variations.result: TRAP: White wins a pawn. Black loses the d5 pawn because the queen can no longer defend it.

moves_from_branch.why: MISTAKE: Looks like it defends e5, but blocks the queen's view of d5.
Simply capture the d5 pawn. Black's queen cannot recapture because the Bd6 is in the way.
Try to recover, but White is up a clean pawn.
Push forward with an extra pawn and a strong center.

sub_variations.name: 3...d5 Trap: The dxe4 Blunder
sub_variations.description: Black captures on e4, but the pin on the c6 knight means White wins a pawn with Nxe5.
sub_variations.result: TRAP: White wins a pawn. The pinned knight cannot defend e5.

sub_variations.name: 3...d5 Gambit: 4...Bd7 (Black's Counter-Trick)
sub_variations.description: Black develops the bishop and offers a gambit with Nd4 discovered attack ideas. White must play accurately.
sub_variations.result: White is up a pawn but Black has active compensation. White should play precisely.

variations.name: The d6 Trap: 5.d5 Ne7 6.Nxe5 d6?? (Devastating Beginner Blunder)
variations.description: In the main line after 3...Nf6 4.d4 Nxe4 5.d5 Ne7 6.Nxe5, Black plays the natural-looking d6 to kick the knight, but walks into a devastating Bb5+ check that wins material. Extremely common at the 700 level.
variations.result: TRAP: White wins at least a pawn with a crushing position. One of the most common Ponziani blunders.

moves_from_base.why: Attack the e4 pawn.
Seize the center.

Kick the c6 knight.

variations.name: Bc5 Trap: 5.d5 Bc5?? (Losing a Piece)

variations.result: TRAP: White wins a piece. The queen attacks both the Nc6 and the Ne4 simultaneously.

variations.result: White wins material with precise play. The line is very sharp but favors White.

moves_from_base.why: Develop the knight to e7. Solid but passive -- blocks the f8 bishop.
Push forward in the center as planned.
Capture the d4 pawn.
Recapture. White has a strong d4-e4 pawn center.
Strike at the center.
Capture.
Recapture with the knight centrally.
Develop the bishop to an active square, targeting d5 and f7.
Develop the bishop.
Castle to safety. White has active pieces and a central pawn on d4.
Castle.
Place the rook on the open e-file. White has natural pressure and a harmonious position.

variations.full_pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. c3 Nge7 4. d4 exd4 5. cxd4 d5 6. exd5 Nxd5 7. Bc4 Be7 8. O-O O-O 9. Re1

beginner_tips: Always play d4 after c3 -- that's the whole point of the Ponziani.
After 3...d5, always play 4.Qa4 to pin the knight. This is where most of the traps come from.
If Black plays passively (d6, Be7, a6), just develop normally: Bd3, O-O, Nbd2, Nf1, Ng3. You'll get a great position.
The Nxf2 trap after 7.Bd3 is the most famous Ponziani trap -- learn it by heart.
If Black plays 3...d5 4.Qa4 Bd6?? -- just take 5.exd5 for a free pawn.
If Black plays 3...d5 4.Qa4 dxe4?? -- play 5.Nxe5 and win a pawn since the knight is pinned.
After 5.d5 Ne7 6.Nxe5, if Black plays d6?? -- play 7.Bb5+ and win material after dxc6, Nxc6.
After 5.d5, if Black plays Bc5?? -- play 6.Qa4+ and win a piece with the double attack on Nc6 and Ne4.
If Black plays 3...Bc5, just play 4.d4 and the bishop must move again -- you gain a free tempo.
If Black brings the queen out early (3...Qf6 or 3...Qe7), play 4.d4 and develop quickly. The queen will be in the way.
After the main line 8.O-O, White's plan is Re1, Nbd2, Nf1-g3 for a kingside attack. The d5 pawn gives you lasting space.
